Building a Homelab with Flux GitOps: Lessons from Three Months

I've been running a homelab for a few years, but this March I decided to do it properly. Talos Linux on a Mac Mini, Flux for GitOps, SOPS for secrets, and a homepage dashboard that shows everything at a glance.

Three months in, I have a working cluster with Immich, Plex, Grafana, CrowdSec, Traefik, and a handful of other services. More importantly, I have documentation that actually compounds — each problem I solved made the next one easier.
Here's what I built, what broke, and how I'll do it again.
— Immutable, API-driven Kubernetes. No SSH, no shell, no apt-get. Sounds limiting until you realize you never need to debug "what changed on the node."
— GitOps controller. Everything lives in a git repo, Flux reconciles it to the cluster. Push a change, it lands. Revert a change, it's gone.
— Encrypted secrets in git. No plaintext, no sealed-secrets complexity. Decrypt with your age key, apply, done.
— Dashboard with widgets for every service. Internal URLs, API tokens, done.
— Ingress with ACME DNS challenges for
— Bouncer + LAPI for intrusion detection at the edge.
Storage Classes Matter More Than You Think
) for everything. Loki crashed in a persistent loop with
The problem: NFS lacks POSIX fsync/locking semantics. Databases need them.
for databases, NFS for everything else. One decision fixed a week of crash loops.
The cluster went through four scheduling strategies:
Worker node only — simple, single point of failure
All nodes with control-plane tolerations — apps ran on control plane, messy
Monitoring on control plane — freed worker resources when I ran out
: Apps on workers. Monitoring on control plane. Documented exceptions in HelmRelease comments.

Getting Traefik to proxy Synology DSM/Drive required three specific fixes:
— DSM's WebSocket-based UI and asset loading break with request inspection middleware
— Some DSM builds have HTTP/2 bugs causing connection failures

apiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1
kind: ServersTransport
metadata:
  name: synology-insecure
  namespace: traefik
spec:
  insecureSkipVerify: true
  disableHTTP2: true
If you ever have ~50% 502s on a route, check for mixed Endpoints + EndpointSlice:
language-bash shiki shiki-themes material-theme-lighter github-light github-dark monokai
kubectl get endpoints -n traefik synology-dsm  # Delete if present
Manually created Endpoints spawn a mirrored EndpointSlice via
. Delete the Endpoints object and rely on Git-managed EndpointSlice only.
Initial VoidAuth setup had 700-900ms latency on unauthenticated requests. Root cause:
caused internal requests to hairpin NAT — out to the public IP and back.
SOPS with Age keys is dead simple. One gotcha: there's no

# Decrypt
SOPS_AGE_KEY_FILE=~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt sops -d file.sops.yaml > /tmp/plain.yaml
# Edit
vim /tmp/plain.yaml
# Re-encrypt (filename must match .sops.yaml path regex)
cp /tmp/plain.yaml /tmp/plain.yaml.sops.yaml
SOPS_AGE_KEY_FILE=~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt sops --config .sops.yaml -e /tmp/plain.yaml.sops.yaml > file.sops.yaml
Annoying but reliable. Documented it once, never had to figure it out again.
Once you understand the rules, Homepage is great:
mounts don't hot-reload — Reloader handles this automatically
— When updating Homepage secrets, don't patch individual fields. Delete and recreate the secret to ensure all credentials are present:

kubectl delete secret -n homepage homepage-secrets
kubectl apply -f new-secret.yaml  # With ALL credentials from SOPS
kubectl rollout restart -n homepage deployment/homepage
This prevents the "missing credentials" issue where widgets show 401 errors because only some secrets were updated.
Widgets for Immich, Grafana, Traefik, CrowdSec, Plex, and Synology all working.
The workflow is muscle memory now:

# Edit the repo
# Commit and push
git add -A && git commit -m "feat: add thing" && git push

# Reconcile
flux reconcile kustomization apps --with-source

# Verify
kubectl get helmrelease -A
Flux has a built-in web UI (provided by the flux-operator) that shows the status of all GitOps resources at a glance. It's accessible at
— Visual indicators for each Kustomization and HelmRelease
— Click into any object to see its spec and status
— Quickly spot what's failing and why
— See recent reconciliation attempts and outcomes

spec:
  values:
    instance:
      web:
        enabled: true
        domain: flux.example.com
This deploys the flux-operator UI service in the
(typically 8080) and is then exposed externally via Traefik IngressRoute:

apiVersion: traefik.io/v1alpha1
kind: IngressRoute
metadata:
  name: flux-ui
  namespace: flux-system
spec:
  entryPoints:
    - websecure
  routes:
    - kind: Rule
      match: "Host(`flux.example.com`)"
      services:
        - name: flux-operator
          port: http-web
The UI is particularly helpful when debugging stuck reconciliations or understanding why a HelmRelease isn't deploying. It complements the CLI workflow for day-to-day operations.
In the anonymized config, this is exposed via Traefik IngressRoute to
Four custom dashboards were built to make monitoring actionable:
— Single-pane view of cluster health: CPU/memory gauges for control plane and worker nodes, disk usage, and key service status
— Deep dive into Kubernetes resource health, pod counts, and namespace-level metrics
— Request rates, response times, error rates, and service-level metrics for all ingress traffic
— Photo library metrics: upload rates, storage usage, transcoding performance, and user activity
Each dashboard is deployed as a ConfigMap with
label, auto-discovered by Grafana. They're maintained in
Standard Kubernetes dashboards show raw metrics; custom dashboards show
services in context. When Immich is slow, you can immediately see if it's storage, transcoding, or database queries.
The most valuable output of this project isn't the cluster — it's the
Every time I solved a non-trivial problem, I documented it: the symptoms, what didn't work, the fix, and why it works. This is the
pattern — each solution documented makes the next one faster.
) encapsulates the patterns from this homelab — Flux reconciliation, SOPS secrets, Homepage widgets, and service-specific gotchas. It's like a runbook that travels with the agent.

When I ask "why is Immich down," it knows to check storage class first, then node scheduling, then Flux kustomization health. It remembers that node names changed, that OCI chart sources have two valid patterns, and that CrowdSec blocks
— Detecting mixed Endpoints + EndpointSlice issues, orphaned services, hardcoded LAN IPs
— Proper sequence for GitRepository → Kustomization → HelmRelease
— SOPS file structure, secret synchronization, validation workflows

) is a structured approach to documentation — researching problems, assembling solutions, writing them down, and validating that they work. Each documented solution makes the next one faster. This pattern applies to any project, not just homelabs.
Getting Started with Flux Homelab Experimentation
If you want to try this yourself:
is great if you want an immutable, API-driven cluster.
is great if you want something more familiar. Either way, you need:
A machine (old laptop, NUC, Mac Mini, VM)
A git repo for your Flux configuration
to install Flux on your cluster, then bootstrap your GitOps repository:
This creates the bootstrap kustomization that points to your
— In v2, Flux components run in
by default. Some older guides reference
— The controller now handles kustomizations natively; you don't need separate
— The bootstrap command needs a token with
permissions. Personal Access Tokens work, but GitHub Apps are recommended for production.
— Use forward slashes even on Windows; Flux paths are Git repository paths, not filesystem paths.
for HelmRelease. OCI-backed charts have two valid patterns:
directly. If one pattern fails, try the other.
). Mixing them incorrectly causes validation errors. Use:

install:
  serverSideApply: false

  force: true

CPU often indicates a failing Helm release in a retry loop.

homelab-infra/
├── clusters/           # Flux bootstrap
├── infrastructure/
│   ├── controllers/    # Traefik, Prometheus, etc.
│   └── configs/        # Routes, monitoring, alerts
└── apps/
    └── my-app/         # namespace, kustomization, helmrelease
Set up age encryption before you have any secrets:

age-keygen -o ~/.config/sops/age/keys.txt
# Add the public key to .sops.yaml
Don't try to deploy everything at once. Start with:
Each step teaches you something. Document each step.
directory in your repo. When you solve something non-trivial, write it down. Use YAML frontmatter so future-you (or your AI assistant) can search by module, tags, and problem type.
The compounding effect is real. Week 1 I spent hours debugging Loki crash loops. Week 12, I checked the documented solution and fixed a similar issue in minutes.
The cluster keeps growing. Next up:
Gitea Actions for CI (self-hosted runners in-cluster)
Synology Drive integration (finally got the Traefik routing right)
Maybe a second node for actual HA
The homelab isn't a project with an end state. It's an ongoing experiment where each iteration teaches something new. The key is capturing those lessons so they compound.
